mission: To enable 'Scale without Weight' by defining a standard for modular features that can be auto-loaded at build-time based on usage. This allows libraries to offer massive capabilities (rich DX) while delivering minimal bundles (optimal UX).

pillars.title: The Contract
pillars.subtitle: Runtime Interface
pillars.desc: Defines how a Trait module interacts with a Host component. Traits are designed as isolated mixins or functional compositions that operate safely on a typed host.
pillars.details: Must support cleanup lifecycles and strictly typed input requirements.

pillars.title: The Map
pillars.subtitle: Static Definition via Web Docs
pillars.desc: Leverages the Manifest Spec to statically declare relationships between Component Props/Attributes and Trait Modules.

pillars.title: The Enforcer
pillars.subtitle: Build-Time Tooling
pillars.desc: Plugins (Vite, Rollup, Webpack) that analyze template or code usage and automatically inject the necessary trait imports.
pillars.details: Eliminates manual 'glue code' for the developer.

map_materialization.title: The Map — Materialization Decision
map_materialization.status: Decided 2026-06-06 — revisit when building The Enforcer.
map_materialization.decision: The Map is materialized as a standalone trait manifest: a single attribute→module table of lazy import() thunks in the reference implementation (e.g. { sortable: () => import('./traits/Sortable') }), applied by a registerTraits() helper that calls CustomAttributeRegistry.defineLazy for each entry. Chosen over folding the map into per-block manifests (a composesTraits field on each block) and over holding it as JSON in the standard repository.
rationale: One global table, one wiring point. A single attribute→module manifest gives one place to see every trait and one call to register them all — mirroring how the other runtime registries are bootstrapped — rather than scattering the map across per-block declarations.

Runtime-only, no build tooling required. The literal import() specifiers are statically analyzable, so a bundler can code-split each trait today, before The Enforcer exists. This makes the runtime lazy path real on its own.

Forward-compatible with The Enforcer. This table is exactly what The Enforcer will later generate from scanned usage, so build tooling slots in by emitting the manifest instead of hand-authoring it — with no change to the runtime contract.
considerations: Source-of-truth tension. Because the manifest values are implementation module paths, the manifest lives in the reference implementation, not the standard. If the standard should later own the Map as data (a JSON manifest under Web Docs — the original framing above), note that JSON cannot carry the literal import() specifiers a bundler needs to split; it would still require a generated thunk table. So a JSON-in-standard source of truth is an Enforcer-era concern, not a runtime one.

Registration must precede upgrade. Like eager define, defineLazy must run before a tree is upgraded — the attribute observer's filter is fixed at upgrade time. registerTraits() therefore runs during bootstrap, ahead of the first upgrade.
Default delivery is decided (see The Delivery Dimension below): per-trait delivery: eager | lazy, defaulting to lazy, with eager as a manifest-level override.

delivery_dimension.title: The Delivery Dimension — eager vs. lazy
delivery_dimension.decision: Trait delivery is a per-trait configurable dimension, not a fixed mechanic: a declared trait is either lazy (code-split into its own chunk and loaded on first DOM appearance) or eager (baked into the main bundle and applied synchronously). Both are legitimate end-states — build decides the split, runtime decides the load — so the dimension is exposed rather than one global behaviour being baked in.
delivery_dimension.default: Default: lazy. A trait declared with no explicit delivery is code-split and defineLazy'd, loaded on first appearance — the "Scale without Weight" mission as the path of least resistance. Pop-in on first appearance is acceptable because traits are progressive enhancement: the base element is functional without the trait.
delivery_dimension.override: Override: eager, declared on the trait-manifest entry (the trait author owns it — they know whether the trait is tiny, always-on, or first-paint-critical). An eager trait is statically imported into the main bundle and define'd up front, so it applies synchronously with no separate request and no pop-in. The runtime already supports this — an eager define supersedes a pending lazy registration — so the override is near-free.
rationale: The default serves the mission. Lazy-by-default means every trait pays its weight only when used; the bundle stays minimal without the author thinking about it.

The override serves the exceptions. A tiny or first-paint-critical trait (e.g. one that prevents a flash of unstyled content) is better baked in — an on-demand fetch would add a request and visible pop-in for no bundle-size win. eager hands that call to the trait author.

A per-usage override exists too (see The Per-usage Override below): a page author can preload a normally-lazy trait at a specific placement with <trait>-delivery="eager" — warming the chunk at bootstrap rather than on first appearance.

usage_override.title: The Per-usage Override — page-author preload
usage_override.decision: Where the manifest entry lets the trait author set delivery globally, a page author can override it at a single placement with the reserved <trait>-delivery="eager" attribute — e.g. <ul sortable sortable-delivery="eager">. The suffix binds the override unambiguously to the named trait and never collides with the trait's own value slot (sortable="desc" still works).
usage_override.semantics: The override is eager-only and build-time. It cannot move code between bundles — where a trait is bundled is frozen at build time — so it only changes when an already-split chunk loads. eager at a usage site therefore means preload this chunk: the Enforcer scans the template source (including not-yet-mounted views), marks the lazy trait preload, and registerTraits warms it at bootstrap via CustomAttributeRegistry.preload instead of waiting for first appearance.
usage_override.scope: Only the eager direction is exposed. For the initial DOM every trait already loads at bootstrap (the observer fires on upgrade() for everything present, hidden or not — it keys on DOM presence, not visibility), so the override's real value is warming a trait whose element mounts later (a route/view not yet rendered) — the one case the runtime DOM cannot see but the build-time scan can. The inverse (forcing an eager trait lazy at a usage site) is deliberately not exposed: it yields no bundle-size win, only an apply-timing change, so by the dimension-vs-fixed-mechanic rule it stays a fixed mechanic.
usage_override.production_note: The reference runtime preloads via an eager import() at bootstrap (works in dev and prod, consistent with the runtime-first design). A build that wants the earliest possible fetch can additionally emit <link rel="modulepreload"> for the chunk into <head> — the same production-layer pattern as the eager dimension's "no separate request" (proven at the codegen layer).

inert_dead_zone.title: The inert Dead-Zone — a behaviour-activation scope
inert_dead_zone.decision: A native inert subtree is a behaviour-activation boundary: interaction-driven traits inside it go dormant by default. inert is reused as the dead-zone primitive rather than inventing a parallel marker — the platform already defines it as a non-interactive subtree (removed from the tab order, click handling, and the assistive-technology interaction surface), it inherits down the tree, and authors get the dead-zone for free. The related terms aria-disabled (semantically disabled, still perceivable) and aria-hidden (removed from the a11y tree) are documented-but-distinct — behaviours do not key off them.
inert_dead_zone.scoping: Auto-dormant, scoped by meaning — not a blanket "all behaviours stop". A trait declares its activation surface. An interaction-driven behaviour (sortable drag, dropdown open, a click handler) is definitionally meaningless inside inert, so it deactivates automatically. An ambient behaviour that runs independent of interaction (autosave, polling, a live clock) isn't addressed by inert's semantics at all, so it is left running — resolving the "autosave shouldn't stop because a preview is shown" counterexample by meaning rather than a flag. The native-first default is interaction (a live interaction behaviour in an inert region is usually a bug); ambient is the deliberate opt-out.
inert_dead_zone.override: Per-usage re-enable: the reserved <trait>-active attribute (presence re-enables), matching the <trait>-delivery suffix convention (#202). <ul sortable sortable-active> keeps sortable's behaviour activated inside an inert region. The suffix binds to the named trait, dodges the value slot (sortable="desc") and the delivery override, and a bare unscoped keep-active is rejected (which trait?). Unlike <trait>-delivery (build-time), <trait>-active is read from the live DOM at runtime. It is the disable-counterpart to #202's per-usage enable: the scope disables, the placement re-enables.
inert_dead_zone.lifecycle: Connected ≠ activated. A behaviour has two orthogonal states: connected (in the DOM) and activated (should run). connectedCallback/disconnectedCallback stay about DOM presence; a new activatedCallback()/deactivatedCallback() pair (named for the platform's connectedCallback convention) carries the start/stop-running work — e.g. attaching interaction listeners. The dead-zone toggles the activated state via a MutationObserver watching inert (and inherited ancestor inert), never touching connectedness. This activation lifecycle is shared infrastructure: the visibility gate (#221) consumes the same contract.
rationale: Native-first, no invented jargon. Grounding the dead-zone in the platform's own inert — an interaction-level dead zone with established focus/click/AT semantics — means authors reuse a concept they already know, and the scope is exactly the platform's notion of "non-interactive," not a bespoke one.

The scripting complement to inert's native block. inert already stops pointer/keyboard events from reaching the subtree; the activation gate is the scripting analogue — it stops the behaviour's own machinery (listeners, drag managers, interaction-started timers) and gives a clean activate/deactivate contract that composes with the visibility gate (#221) and survives inert flipping live.

Scoped by meaning resolves the autosave bug. Because the surface is declared per trait, an ambient behaviour is simply outside inert's remit and keeps running; no "stop everything" rule has to carve out exceptions.
One override vocabulary. <trait>-active reuses the same per-usage suffix shape as <trait>-delivery, so page authors learn one rule for placement-level overrides.

visibility_gate.title: The Visibility Gate — activation on first view
visibility_gate.decision: A trait can declare that it activates when its host becomes visible (enters the viewport) rather than the moment it is connected. connectedCallback (in the DOM) is not a sufficient activation signal — collapsed accordions, inactive tabs, display:none modals and far-below-the-fold sections are present but not yet relevant. The gate is the scripting analogue of content-visibility: auto (which already skips off-screen rendering) and is driven by the platform's own IntersectionObserver — native vocabulary, not an invented one.
visibility_gate.shape: Same two-layer shape as delivery (#202) and the inert dead-zone (#222): a trait-author manifest default plus a page-author per-usage override. The default is the status quo — activate-on-connect — so the gate is strictly opt-in and nothing existing changes behaviour. The override is the reserved <trait>-when="visible" attribute — e.g. <section reveal reveal-when="visible">. when is a general activation-trigger axis whose first value is visible (leaving room for idle or media-query triggers later); it is read from the live DOM at runtime, like <trait>-active (#222) and unlike the build-time <trait>-delivery (#202). The suffix binds to the named trait, dodging the value slot (reveal="fade"), the delivery override and the inert re-enable.
visibility_gate.semantics: What "dormant" means falls out of composing with delivery — it is not a separate choice. Visibility-gated + lazy delivery = fetch-on-view: the chunk is not even import()ed until the host intersects — the real bundle/runtime win the presence-keyed observer cannot deliver (it upgrades everything in the tree at upgrade(), visible or not). Visibility-gated + eager delivery = apply-on-view: the code is already bundled, so only construction/activation defers until intersection. "Dormant" therefore means not-yet-activated — no instance for lazy, constructed-but-inactive for eager — never a half-running state.
visibility_gate.reactivation: The re-entry policy is derived from the existing activation surface axis (#222), not declared a third time. An ambient behaviour (polling, a live clock) defaults to recurring: it deactivates on exit and re-activates on re-entry — pausing off-screen is the entire point. An interaction behaviour (drag, click wiring) defaults to once: it activates on first view and stays, because tearing down interaction wiring every time the host scrolls out of view is surprising and janky. A trait author can override the policy where a behaviour does not fit its surface's default. The gate toggles the shared activatedCallback()/deactivatedCallback() lifecycle (#223) via an IntersectionObserver, exactly as the dead-zone toggles it via a MutationObserver on inert — the two activation gates compose.
rationale: Native-first, no invented jargon. IntersectionObserver is the platform's own "is this in view" primitive and content-visibility: auto already gates rendering on the same signal; the visibility gate is just the scripting half of a mechanic the platform already endorses.

The natural companion to lazy delivery. delivery governs when a trait's code arrives; the gate governs when it runs. Together (lazy + visible) they answer the case raised against the lazy default — the submerged iceberg of in-DOM-but-hidden content that otherwise loads at bootstrap regardless.
One override vocabulary. <trait>-when reuses the same per-usage suffix shape as <trait>-delivery and <trait>-active, so page authors learn one rule for placement-level overrides.

Reuses the activation axis instead of inventing one. Deriving the re-entry policy from interaction/ambient is the same move #222 made by leaning on inert rather than a bespoke marker — fewer concepts, and the two gates share one activate/deactivate contract.

tech_details.title: Reference Implementation: The Assertion Pattern
tech_details.warning: ✅ Verified Proof of Concept: This pattern ensures type safety without global namespace pollution.

steps.title: 1. The Pure Component
steps.desc: The host component remains lightweight and unaware of its potential traits. It only contains core logic.

steps.title: 2. The Trait Definition
steps.desc: Defines the interface, the mixin logic, and a special 'Assertion Loader' that upgrades the type safely.

steps.title: 3. The Mixin Logic
steps.desc: The implementation details. It extends the instance dynamically, bridging the gap between the clean component and the feature.

steps.title: 4. Strict Consumption
steps.desc: TypeScript prevents using the trait until you explicitly load it. No more runtime crashes from missing methods.

steps.title: 5. Opt-in Automation (Future)
steps.desc: While explicit loading is safe, build plugins can opt-in to analyze triggers (props, unique methods) and inject the loader automatically.
